title: Bṛhad Atichāra — The Extended Confession of the Twelve Lay Vows' Transgressions (Moṭā Atiyār)
summary: This is the long, detailed confession a Jain lay person (śrāvaka) recites to review the twelve vows of a householder and to admit, vow by vow, every way he or she has fallen short of them. After each vow it lists the standard 'slips' (atichāra) and then spells out, in everyday language and with very concrete examples, the careless or wrong acts that may have happened. Each section closes with 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' — 'may that wrong of mine be undone' — turning the catalogue into an act of repentance. It is recited in the longer pratikramaṇas (fortnightly, seasonal, and yearly) where there is time to examine conduct in full.
ritualPlacement: This is the Fourth Āvaśyaka (Paḍikkamaṇa — reflection and atonement) in its EXTENDED form. It replaces the short confession (laghu-atiyar) used in the daily Devasi/Rāi pratikramaṇas and is recited only in the Pākṣika (Pakkhī, fortnightly), Cāturmāsika (Cāumāsī, season-end), and Sāṁvatsarika (Saṁvatsarī, annual) pratikramaṇas, where the period being reviewed is long enough to warrant the full atichāra. SHARED-SUTRA NOTE (stated once here): the book gives Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī no independent text — they reuse the entire Pākṣika sequence (this sūtra included) with only a word-substitution (પક્ખી 'pakkhī' → ચઉમાસી 'cāumāsī' → સંવત્સરિ 'saṁvatsarī') and a longer kāyotsarga (12 → 20 → 40 Logassa + 1 Navkar). So when a verse says 'in this fortnight' (pakṣa), read 'in these four months' / 'in this year' in the longer observances. The per-verse commentaries rely on this and will not repeat it.
authorAndSource: No individual author is named; like the other atichāra confessions this is a traditional, anonymous devotional composition transmitted within the gaccha. The text used here is the recension printed in the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha Gaccha) Śvetāmbara Mūrtipūjaka edition of 'Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit' (પંચ પ્રતિક્રમણ સૂત્ર – વિધિ સહિત), which is the golden source for every reading. Note that, unlike the Prakrit/Ardhamāgadhī core sūtras, the body of this confession is in old (medieval) Gujarati prose, into which the canonical Prakrit atichāra-formulas (e.g. 'bandhe, vahe, chavicchede, aibhāre, bhattapāṇavucchee') are quoted and then glossed. Authorship and exact date are uncertain (see notes).
structureArc: The confession is built as a fixed, repeating template applied vow by vow. It opens (in v.1) not with a vow but with the ROOT — samyaktva (right faith), called 'samyaktva-mūla', the foundation of all twelve vows: it names the four kinds of mithyātva (false belief — laukika/lokottara × deva-gata/guru-gata), lists samyaktva's own five atichāras (śaṅkā, kāṅkṣā, vitigicchā, para-pāṣaṇḍī-praśaṁsā, para-pāṣaṇḍī-saṁstava), and then a long catalogue of popular/non-Jain observances (worldly festivals, bathing at non-Jain tīrthas, pilgrimages, eclipses, the named loka-parvas) that a śrāvaka may have wrongly kept. Then, still within v.1, it turns to the FIRST aṇuvrata. The five verses provided map to: samyaktva-mūla + v.1 = 1st vow, Prāṇātipāta-viramaṇa (non-violence); v.2 = 2nd vow, (sthūla) Mṛṣāvāda-viramaṇa (truthfulness); v.3 = 3rd vow, (sthūla) Adattādāna-viramaṇa (non-stealing); v.4 = 4th vow, Śīla / Svadāra-santoṣa (chastity); v.5 = 5th vow, Parigraha-parimāṇa (limiting possessions). These five are the five aṇuvratas; the complete bṛhad atichāra continues beyond the supplied verses with the three guṇavratas and four śikṣāvratas (see notes).
recurringImagery: There is little figurative imagery; the unifying device is a STRUCTURAL FORMULA, explained here once so the per-verse commentaries need not re-describe it. For each vow the text moves through four fixed stages: (1) NAME the vow and its scope, often with the practitioner's specific resolve (niyama) and the qualifier 'jayaṇā' (a guarding/care-exception for unavoidable acts); (2) the refrain 'e ... vrat-taṇā pāñca atiyār śodhuṁ' — 'I now examine the five transgressions of this vow'; (3) QUOTE the canonical Prakrit list of five atichāra-names, then GLOSS each in Gujarati with strikingly concrete domestic, agricultural and commercial examples (binding cattle, overloading pack-animals, false weights, adulterating ghee, etc.); (4) the SEALING refrain 'aneruṁ ... je kāṁi ... sūkṣma, bādara atiyār huo hoy, te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' — 'whatever other subtle or gross transgression occurred, all of it, by mind, speech and body, may it be undone.' The triad mind–speech–body (mana–vacana–kāyā) and the pair subtle–gross (sūkṣma–bādara) recur at every seal.
keyThemes: Examination of conscience across all twelve lay vows
Samyaktva (right faith) as the root that the vows grow from
Distinguishing laukika vs lokottara mithyātva (worldly vs misdirected-religious false belief)
The five aṇuvratas: non-violence, truth, non-stealing, chastity, limiting possessions
Confession sealed by mind, speech and body (mana–vacana–kāyā)
Jayaṇā — careful guarding as the lay alternative to total abstention

glossary.definition: A transgression or partial breach of a vow — falling short without fully abandoning it; spelled 'atiyār' (અતિયાર) throughout this book.
A Jain lay follower (householder); feminine śrāvikā. The 'one confessing' here.
A 'lesser vow' — the five basic ethical vows kept by lay people in a limited (sthūla, 'gross/coarse') form, less severe than a monk's great vows.
One of three 'merit/enhancing vows' that reinforce the aṇuvratas (continues beyond the verses supplied here).
One of four 'disciplinary/instructional vows' of lay practice (the Deśāvagāśika is the 10th vow); also beyond the supplied verses.
Right faith or right insight — the foundation ('mūla') on which the twelve vows rest; it has its own five atichāras.
False belief or wrong worldview; here classed four-fold as laukika/lokottara and deva-gata/guru-gata (worldly vs misdirected-religious, regarding deities vs teachers).
Vigilant care or guarding; the qualified exception allowing a householder to act with minimum harm where total avoidance is impossible.
Killing or injuring living beings; the 1st vow is prāṇātipāta-viramaṇa, abstention from such harm.
False or harmful speech; the 2nd vow is abstention from gross lying.
Taking what is not given (theft); the 3rd vow is abstention from gross stealing.
Contentment with one's own spouse and avoidance of others' partners; the substance of the 4th vow (śīla / brahmacarya for laity).
Setting and keeping a limit on one's possessions; the 5th vow, here listed under nine heads of property.
'May my wrong-doing be in vain / undone' — the standard formula of repentance that seals each section of the confession.
Meditative 'abandonment of the body' in stillness, measured in Logassa-counts; the longer observances that use this sūtra prescribe progressively longer kāyotsargas (12 / 20 / 40 Logassa + 1 Navkar).

howToRead: A newcomer can read this as a structured 'examination of conscience': each vow is named, its standard slips are listed, and then very ordinary situations are described so you can honestly ask 'did I do this?' — closing with 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.' An advanced practitioner will attend to the precise Prakrit atichāra-formulas and their medieval-Gujarati glosses, to the four-fold analysis of mithyātva under samyaktva, and to how the substitution rule re-times every 'this fortnight' to the four-month or yearly review.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (પંચ પ્રતિક્રમણ સૂત્ર – વિધિ સહિત), Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha Gaccha edition
sources.consultedFor: The golden source for the verbatim text, its ritual placement as the Fourth Āvaśyaka, and the Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarī substitution rule.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary (jainelibrary.org)
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the general structure of the twelve lay vows (5 aṇuvrata + 3 guṇavrata + 4 śikṣāvrata) and the bṛhad/extended atichāra tradition in Gujarati pratikramaṇa literature.

notes: Caveats: (1) Authorship is anonymous and the date uncertain — treated as a traditional gaccha composition, not attributed; do not infer a named author. (2) The five verses supplied cover samyaktva-mūla plus the five aṇuvratas only; the complete bṛhad atichāra continues with the three guṇavratas and four śikṣāvratas, which are not in the provided text — the per-verse commentaries should not claim those are absent from the sūtra as a whole. (3) The confession's body is in medieval Gujarati prose, with the canonical atichāra-names quoted in Prakrit and then glossed; doctrinal terms are given in IAST here for clarity, but the book's own spellings (notably 'atiyār' અતિયાર, not 'atichāra') are authoritative and preserved verbatim, including internal orthographic inconsistencies. (4) Spaced bars, periods before verse numbers, and footnote variant-readings (pāṭhāntare) in the book are part of the text and not errata.
